answers.value: Small kitchen grease fire originating on the gas range while cooking dinner. Fire was contained quickly but caused smoke damage to upper cabinets, backsplash, and adjacent wall. No injuries. Insured contacted fire department as precaution; fire was extinguished with a home fire extinguisher before significant structural damage occurred. Repairs involved replacing two upper cabinets, repainting the kitchen, and professional smoke and odor remediation throughout the first floor. No prior related losses or negligence indicated.

answers.value:
The insured, Jonathan Michael Carter, is a 43-year-old married software engineering manager who has lived in the home since purchasing it in June 2015. He resides in the property full-time with his spouse, Emily Carter (age 41, high school guidance counselor), and their two children, ages 9 and 6. Both adults are college-educated, financially stable, and have maintained continuous homeowners coverage since purchasing their first home in 2010, with no lapse in coverage at any point.

The dwelling is a well-maintained, two-story, single-family colonial located in a suburban neighborhood in Hingham, MA, approximately 2.5 miles from the nearest fire station and within 1 mile of a fire hydrant. The neighborhood is composed primarily of owner-occupied homes built in the late 1980s to mid-1990s, with a strong homeowners association that enforces property upkeep standards (though the HOA does not provide insurance). The home sits on approximately 0.3 acres with a fenced backyard and a detached wooden shed used exclusively for lawn and garden equipment.

Jonathan and Emily are conscientious homeowners who have invested in several loss-prevention measures. In 2017, after a home inspection identified aging shingles, they had the roof fully replaced by a licensed roofing contractor, adding architectural shingles and additional ice and water shielding to reduce the risk of ice dam-related leaks, which are common in New England winters. In 2019, they remodeled the kitchen and master bathroom, during which licensed plumbers updated several supply lines to modern copper and PEX, and replaced older shutoff valves. In 2020, they replaced the original oil-fired boiler with a high-efficiency natural gas forced-air furnace and added central air conditioning. At that time, a licensed electrician upgraded the main panel to 200 amps and verified that there was no aluminum or knob-and-tube wiring.

The insured has a professionally monitored central station fire and burglar alarm system through a national provider (currently ADT), with smoke and heat detectors on each level and in the basement. The system is tested monthly via the provider’s app. In addition, after a neighbor experienced a significant water loss due to a burst washing machine hose, Jonathan installed a smart automatic water shutoff valve in 2022 that detects abnormal water usage and shuts off the main line remotely or automatically if a leak is detected. There are also individual leak sensors under the kitchen sink, behind the refrigerator, and near the water heater. The insured has multiple ABC-rated fire extinguishers located in the kitchen pantry, garage, and basement stairwell.

Regarding exposures, the only business activity on the premises is Jonathan’s remote software engineering work. He is a W-2 employee of a Boston-based technology company and occasionally works from a dedicated home office on the second floor. This office consists of a desk, two computer monitors, a company-issued laptop, and standard office furniture. No clients or customers visit the home; all meetings are conducted virtually. There is no signage, no inventory, and no special equipment beyond standard electronics. Emily occasionally grades papers and prepares lesson plans at home but does not tutor students at the house, and no students or parents come to the premises for school-related activities.

There are no pets currently in the household. The family previously had a small mixed-breed dog that passed away in 2021; they have not yet adopted another pet. There are no pools, hot tubs, trampolines, or similar attractive nuisances on the property. The backyard contains only a swing set that is anchored and maintained, along with a small garden area. The insured does not own any recreational vehicles such as ATVs, snowmobiles, or personal watercraft. Their only vehicles (two sedans) are insured separately on an auto policy and are garaged or parked in the paved driveway.

The one prior loss in January 2023 was a relatively minor kitchen grease fire that served as a learning experience for the insured. Since that incident, Jonathan and Emily have taken additional precautions: they installed a new range hood with a stronger exhaust fan, keep a lid nearby when cooking to smother any flare-ups, and no longer leave oil unattended on the stove. They also completed a brief online home fire safety course offered by the local fire department and have practiced a family fire evacuation plan with their children, including identifying two ways out of each bedroom and a meeting spot at the end of the driveway.

The home is strictly used as an owner-occupied primary residence. It is never rented out on a short-term basis (such as Airbnb or VRBO), nor is it leased on a long-term basis to tenants. The family typically travels for one week at a time during school vacations and occasionally for a long weekend. When away, they use smart lighting timers, maintain their alarm system, and ask a trusted neighbor to collect mail and visually check the property. In winter, they keep the thermostat no lower than 62°F to reduce the risk of frozen pipes, and they have never experienced a freeze-related loss.

There are no known environmental concerns affecting the property. The home is not located in a designated flood zone that requires mandatory flood insurance, and there is no history of flooding at this address. There is no known presence of asbestos, lead paint, or EIFS on the structure. The siding is vinyl, and prior owners had removed or encapsulated any suspect materials before Jonathan and Emily purchased the home, as documented in the 2015 home inspection report.

In summary, the insureds are stable, low-risk homeowners who proactively maintain their property, invest in safety and loss-mitigation features, and demonstrate responsible habits both in daily living and in response to their prior minor loss. They are seeking comprehensive homeowners coverage with appropriate limits to reflect current reconstruction costs in the Hingham area, along with endorsements such as extended replacement cost, ordinance or law coverage, equipment breakdown, and water backup to address realistic modern loss scenarios they might reasonably face over the life of the policy.
